content:
{
  "title": "Retrieval-Augmented Execution Feedback Prompting for Code Generation",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate syntactically correct but functionally incorrect code, due in part to limited context of problem requirements and execution feedback. Improving functional correctness via better prompting remains an open challenge.",
  "motivation": "Current prompting techniques focus primarily on in-context examples or instruction tuning, but rarely leverage runtime error signals or retrieve relevant code snippets automatically. As coding tasks become more complex, models must reason about execution outcomes and adapt their generations accordingly. Harnessing execution feedback and retrieval in prompts could substantially improve code generation reliability.",
  "method": "We propose a two-stage prompting method: first, the model generates candidate code for a given problem; second, a retrieval module selects similar solved code examples from an external corpus, and the code is executed in a sandbox, capturing any runtime errors or test failures. These retrieved examples and execution traces are then synthesized into a structured feedback message, which is appended as an additional prompt for a second round of code generation. This enables the model to iteratively improve its output using both relevant examples and concrete error diagnostics.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate on HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing against standard few-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. Baselines include retrieval-augmented prompting without execution feedback and automatic self-debugging prompts. Metrics are pass@1 and pass@10 functional correctness. Ablation tests isolate contributions from retrieval and execution feedback."
}
